text: life. It was Arnold van Gennep who would later translate classics such as Westermarck into French; he also translated the eighteen-volume-long series of Havelock Ellis’ works on sexual psychology, with volume I appearing in 1908. He had met Frazer, and was building up a huge knowledge of European anthropology, developing his ideas with and against the French and British schools, but with a keen eye to developments in Germany and the rest of Europe. He had started to publish and was following lectures in some of France’s best departments, performing brilliantly. And then he left. Shortly after his marriage in 1897, he applied for a teaching position at a Polish lycée, in Czentochowa, as a French teacher. The two events were connected and certainly go some way toward explaining van Gennep’s ‘confession’ regarding the significance of Rites of Passage: it seems certain that the ‘ten years of darkness’ for van Gennep referred to both his personal and professional life as it developed from around 1897 to 1908, when the book was finished. Van Gennep’s marriage in 1897 led to a painful break with his mother and his adoptive father, who could not accept the social standing of his chosen one. His wife was young and, judging from the record, very beautiful indeed, but she was also extremely poor, and brought no dowry whatsoever to the marriage. In this as in so many other cases, van Gennep chose to disregard social expectations. He married her because he wanted to. Van Gennep would not really reconcile with his parents again: the break was apparently final, and this certainly must have added to a certain feeling of isolation and loneliness that accompanied his life. The wedding itself was largely devoid of that crucial social recognition by close friends and family that lend rituals of reintegration their significance. 1897 was also the year that van Gennep moved to Russian Poland, attempting to start a career as a school teacher. It was another travel into unknown lands, marital solitude and uncertain futures. It is not entirely clear whether van Gennep had ever been formally adopted by Paul Raugé; and here again the myriad of official encyclopaedic entries are at odds with each other. It seems likely, for it was common custom for men to adopt the children of re-married women, and it is beyond doubt that Paul Raugé to some extent did come to function as Arnold’s father. What we do know with certainty is that up until 1897 van Gennep signed five of his publications as ‘Arnold Raugé van Gennep’ (Ketty van Gennep 1964: 15–16), a clear sign that he recognized Paul as his father, also in the formal sense. After 1897 he would never do so again, 7 nor would he turn to his biological father’s name: from then onwards he would simply call himself ‘Arnold van Gennep’. It is therefore what we rightly call him today. translated’ (Durkheim 1998: 136–7, my translation). There is some likelihood that this is the first time ever that Durkheim stumbled upon the name of Arnold van Gennep (in the letter to Mauss he refers to the ‘two translators’ of Frazer without mentioning their names). If so the ‘relationship’ between Durkheim and van Gennep started out on a somewhat negative note. 7 Ketty van Gennep, in her introduction to the Bibliographie, still bespeaks Paul Raugé as her grandfather. Liminality and the Modern 34 However, we also know that van Gennep continued to hold Paul Raugé in esteem, and there is at least one direct proof of that: his 1906 book, Mythes et Légendes, is dedicated to ‘Dr Paul Raugé’. Why did van Gennep leave France? He was in need of a job; simply to keep on studying was no longer an option. Because of his language skills he was well- qualified to serve France abroad. In Czentochowa he taught himself Russian in eight months. In the examination process for becoming titulaire, he translated from both Latin and Greek into Russian. He stayed there for four years, and published nothing in the period. However, if this job had taken him away from the centre of academic life in France and Europe, he did not exactly waste his time. 8 He taught himself Polish as well, and gained reading efficiency in several other Slavic languages. He read whatever he could get hold off in Slavic languages on myth and religion. He also travelled as much as he could, accompanied by his wife. Rites of Passage contains several references to readings and travels he made in this period. In Czentochowa he was surrounded by an air of suspicion. The French journals he received were checked by the authorities. Europe was a continent of suspicion in the early twentieth century, especially in disputed border areas like Russian Poland. When van Gennep much later, after World War I, developed his own approach to the study of nationalism (van Gennep 1922), it was certainly also as a reflection of his own life experiences within and across Europe’s national boundaries. Van Gennep returned to Paris with his wife in 1901. As indicated by the memoirs of Ketty van Gennep, the return was mainly due to his wife’s pregnancy and precarious health. The return to France would tragically coincide with the loss of their first child. They could not afford good hospitals. Upon his return, and from 1901 to 1908, he was employed as head of translations at the French ministry of Agriculture. His linguistic skills were by then second to none, and in a way that was always going to be his salvation. He practically mastered all Germanic, Latin and Slavic languages. He did not speak the Scandinavian languages, but could read them. And he engaged further with the ‘Oriental languages’ as well. In an article from 1927, discussing the use of the subconscious in the study of living languages, he mentions having mastered eighteen languages plus a number of their dialects (Belmont 1979: 7). 9 8 Without overstressing the parallel, it is worth
